ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for chilli-oil dressed cucumber specifically; closest parent 'cucumber food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cucumber and gherkins as a category; no specific code exists for marinated chilli-oil dressed cucumber preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 412061001 identifies cucumber as a substance; no specific concept exists for the dressed/marinated preparation with chilli oil and garlic.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of cucumber dressed with garlic and chilli oil is approximately 150 g, presented as a cold appetiser or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Cucumber, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11206) combined with chilli oil dressing component estimation from USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for chilli-oil dressed cucumber exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy raw cucumber (NDB #11206, ~15 kcal/100 g) with a proportional contribution from chilli oil dressing (approximately 20–25 g dressing per 100 g finished dish, contributing oil, garlic, soy sauce, and seasoning). The resulting composite estimate reflects a typical restaurant-style preparation. Confidence is medium due to estimation. HPB Singapore Foodo was checked first; no matching entry was found for this specific dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for cucumber
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw cucumber has a GI of approximately 15 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The chilli oil dressing adds negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 6.3) / 100 ≈ 0.9, rounded to 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning in the chilli oil dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the soy sauce and seasoning in the chilli oil dressing contribute approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g; portion control to one small serving (≤150 g) is advisable to limit sodium intake to around 630 mg per occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and only 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a low-carbohydrate vegetable side, though the added oil increases caloric density and should be factored into total energy targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat from chilli oil with low saturated fat and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The chilli oil dressing provides predominantly unsaturated fatty acids (monounsaturated and polyunsaturated) with low saturated fat (0.9 g/100 g) and no cholesterol, making this dish acceptable for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderate portions; however, the moderate sodium level warrants attention in patients with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cucumber Dressed with Garlic and Chilli Oil (辣味黄瓜) is a chilled, marinated vegetable appetiser of Chinese origin, popularised in Singapore through restaurant chains such as Din Tai Fung. The dish consists of fresh mini cucumbers — typically smashed or sliced — that are marinated in a fragrant dressing of chilli oil, sliced garlic, sesame oil, soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, and dried chilli flakes. The preparation method involves chilling the cucumbers after marination to allow the flavours to penetrate, resulting in a crisp, refreshing texture with a bold, spicy, garlicky, and slightly tangy flavour profile. Nutritionally, the dish is low in carbohydrates (~4.2 g/100 g) and has a very low glycemic index (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The fat content (~5.8 g/100 g) comes primarily from the chilli oil and is predominantly unsaturated. Sodium is moderate (~420 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce in the dressing, which is a consideration for those managing hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a cold starter or side dish alongside dumplings, noodles, or rice in Chinese and Taiwanese-style restaurants across Singapore. The dish is closely related to the Sichuan-style smashed cucumber salad (拍黄瓜, pāi huángguā) tradition.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for chilli-oil dressed cucumber was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy raw cucumber data with a proportional chilli oil dressing component. GI is based on raw cucumber GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is a defensible analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. food_group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables', as cucumber is a vegetable and this dish is a vegetable preparation. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as the dish contains no hydrogenated fats; this is naturally zero for a plant-based oil and vegetable preparation. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal products. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no fortification and no UV-exposed mushrooms. 6. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy cucumber entry used for estimation and the dressing component does not have a reliable reported value. 7. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite estimation source. 8. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite estimation source. 9. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite estimation source. 10. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the composite estimation source. 11. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite estimation source. 12. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite estimation source. 13. Halal status is assumed based on plant-based ingredients; restaurant-level halal certification was not verified from an approved source. 14. This dish is classified as Chinese cuisine (Singaporean restaurant context); multilingual names for English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided per the Singaporean dish multilingual rule.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Grains and staples' (input error) to 'Vegetables'.
Built full multilingual local_names for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi per Singaporean dish rule.
Estimated nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy cucumber + chilli oil dressing component since no exact HPB or primary regional source entry exists.
Set GI to 15 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database raw cucumber analogous estimate.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values with zero justification phrases in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Set ontology match_type values to allowed values only: closest_parent for all three ontology systems.
Included Type 2 Diabetes with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' and machine key type2_diabetes.
